coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of KCl-doped lignin carbon dots nanoenzymes for colorimetric sensing glutathione in human serum
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Xiaoyu
Sun, Qijun
Ma, Yanmei
Jiang, Xinxin
Niu, Na
Chen, Ligang
coredata.dc:description: A KCl-doped lignin carbon dots (K,Cl-LCDs) nanoenzymes was successfully synthesized by hydrothermal method to construct a sensor for detecting glutathione (GSH). Sodium lignosulfonate with a lot of oxygen-containing functional groups as a carbon precursor can provide effective active sites, and KCl as a dopant can influence the charge distribution on the surface of CDs. In the enzyme catalyzing progress, K,Cl-LCDs with peroxidase-like activity can oxidize 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into blue oxTMB with the presence of H2O2, the absorbance of the system at 652 nm changed consequently. When GSH is introduced into the system, the reducibility of GSH restores oxTMB to colorless. So that, a sensor for colorimetric detection of GSH is constructed. In addition, through free radical experiments, the catalytic mechanism was explored, confirming that the reaction was induced by hydroxyl radicals (·OH). The sensor has a low detection limit (0.08 μM) for detecting GSH with good linear range (0.25–25 μM). The sensor system was applied to the determination of GSH concentration in human serum samples with the recoveries of 98.2%− 103.2% and RSDs of 2.3%− 4.7%. The proposed sensor for GSH detection has the advantages of simple operation, low cost, and high speed, indicating its potential application in bioanalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignin
Carbon dots
Peroxidase activity
Nanoenzyme
Glutathione
Colorimetric sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of K,Cl-LCDs by hydrothermal method
Colorimetric determination of GSH with K,Cl-LCDs
Assay detection of GSH in serum samples
Characterization of the inherent peroxidase K,Cl-LCDs
Peroxidase mimetic behavior of K,Cl-LCDs and sensing properties optimization
Analysis of steady-state kinetic studies of K,Cl-LCDs
Study on the catalytic mechanism of peroxidase-like
Catalytic stability study of K,Cl-LCDs and selective research
Detection of spiked GSH in human serum samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Tree Genetics and Breeding Innovation Team

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was funded by Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( 2572021BU03 ), 111 Project ( B20088 ), Heilongjiang Touyan Innovation Team Program (Tree Genetics and Breeding Innovation Team).

This project was funded by Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572021BU03), 111 Project (B20088), Heilongjiang Touyan Innovation Team Program (Tree Genetics and Breeding Innovation Team). All applicable international, national, and/or institutional guidelines for the collection and use of human blood and serum samples were followed. All biological experiments were approved by the Ethics Committee of Harbin Medical University Cancer Hospital.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A). TEM and HRTEM images of K,Cl-LCDs. (B). Histogram of particle size distribution of K,Cl-LCDs. (C). FTIR spectra of K,Cl-LCDs. (D). XRD patterns of K,Cl-LCDs.
(A). EDS spectra of K,Cl-LCDs. (B). Excitation emission spectra of K,Cl-LCDs. (C). PL spectra of K,Cl-LCDs at different pH (D). The effect of ionic strength on the PL of K,Cl-LCDs.
(A). Effects of different free radical scavengers. (B). Hydroxyl Radical Spectrum of TA. (C). Change of absorbance with volume of IPA. (D). ESR spectra of different systems. (E). Potential analysis.
Diagram of GSH detection. (A). GSH detection time optimization. (B). The system absorbance change with GSH volume. (C). Linear schematic diagram of detecting GSH system.
Strategy of synthesizing K,Cl-LCDs and detecting for GSH.
Test results of actual human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of KCl-doped lignin carbon dots nanoenzymes for colorimetric sensing glutathione in human serum

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Northeast Forestry University, 26 Hexing Road, Harbin 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Northeast Forestry University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Northeast Forestry University, 26 Hexing Road, Harbin 150040, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Xiaoyu Xu and Qijun Sun contributed equally to this work

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A KCl-doped lignin carbon dots (K,Cl-LCDs) nanoenzymes was successfully synthesized by hydrothermal method to construct a sensor for detecting glutathione (GSH). Sodium lignosulfonate with a lot of oxygen-containing functional groups as a carbon precursor can provide effective active sites, and KCl as a dopant can influence the charge distribution on the surface of CDs. In the enzyme catalyzing progress, K,Cl-LCDs with peroxidase-like activity can oxidize 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into blue oxTMB with the presence of H2O2, the absorbance of the system at 652 nm changed consequently. When GSH is introduced into the system, the reducibility of GSH restores oxTMB to colorless. So that, a sensor for colorimetric detection of GSH is constructed. In addition, through free radical experiments, the catalytic mechanism was explored, confirming that the reaction was induced by hydroxyl radicals (·OH). The sensor has a low detection limit (0.08 μM) for detecting GSH with good linear range (0.25–25 μM). The sensor system was applied to the determination of GSH concentration in human serum samples with the recoveries of 98.2%− 103.2% and RSDs of 2.3%− 4.7%. The proposed sensor for GSH detection has the advantages of simple operation, low cost, and high speed, indicating its potential application in bioanalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH) is a tripeptide made up of glutamic acid, cysteine, and glycine [1] that has been found throughout the body. GSH is a strong antioxidant and antidote that plays a critical function in managing the redox state of cells. Furthermore, GSH has been linked to major disorders such as diabetes, Alzheimer's disease, and cancer [2]. At present, the efficient detection of GSH is still being explored, which is of great significance for the early diagnosis and treatment of the above-mentioned diseases. The currently developed GSH detection methods mainly include capillary electrophoresis [3], electrochemical method [4], mass spectrometry surface enhanced Raman scattering method [5], high performance liquid chromatography [6] and so on. Although some of these technologies have a high sensitivity, the majority of them have a long pretreatment time, a difficult operation, and a high cost, making speedy detection clinically impossible [7]. At the same time, fluorescence and colorimetric methods are simple, rapid and sensitive, hence they have wider applications. Among them, fluorescence methods tend to use the excitation emission nature, which require the excitation of energy light [8]. For example, Neeraj Sohal et al. constructed a biosensor for GSH detection based on the composites of carbon dots (CDs) and different morphologies MnO2 [9,10]. Sensitive detection of GSH was achieved through energy resonance transfer between CDs, GSH, and different shapes of MnO2 nanostructures. While colorimetric methods are simpler to operate and can be distinguished with the naked eye. In the colorimetric methods, the use of nanoenzymes to obtain efficient analytical assays is the direction of scientists' research [11–13].

Nanoenzymes are a class of substances that have the unique properties of nanomaterials and can simulate the activity of natural enzymes [14]. Compared with natural enzymes, nanoenzymes have the advantages of high catalytic activity, excellent stability, and economic feasibility [15]. Nanoenzymes can overcome the inherent shortcomings of natural enzymes and provide the possibility for applications in different scenarios. Therefore, the preparation of nanoenzymes has been extensively studied. Over the past decade or so, many research groups have developed nanomaterials with peroxidase activity, including metalloids, carbon-based nanomaterials, and metal-organic framework compounds (MOFs) [16]. Previous articles have reported many synthesis methods and applications of carbon-based nanoenzymes [17], but they all have some inherent defects. For example, the precursors used in the synthesis of nanoenzymes are mostly toxic organics and precious metal, the synthesis methods are complicated and the cost is relatively high. Recent years, the rapid development of low-cost and environmentally friendly synthesis of carbon-based nanoenzymes has attracted many researchers. Carbon-based nanoenzymes mainly include carbon nanotubes [18], CDs [19], graphite-phase carbon nitride [20], graphene oxide [21], hollow carbon spheres [22], mesoporous carbon [23], etc. CDs have gained a lot of attention among these carbon-based nanoenzymes due to their simple manufacturing techniques, low toxicity, high stability, and chemical inertness [24]. The photoluminescence characteristics (PL) of CDs have been studied extensively. For example, Shagun Kainth et al. used different nitrogen-rich precursors and single precursor surfactants containing heteroatoms to prepare CDs, so that the surface state and emission properties of CDs were changed. Therefore, CDs could achieve the detection of creatinine and fluoride ions using PL performance. They also studied the PL properties of CDs in solvents of different polarities, revealed the changing trend of emission wavelength with solvent, and realized the sensing of various metal ions [25–27]. However, there are few reports on alkali metal doping to enhance the catalytic performance of CDs.

Lignin is the second largest renewable and sustainable resource in plants [28]. Only about 10% of the world's industrial lignin can be used effectively each year, the transformation value is relatively low. The high-level utilization of lignin needs to be further developed. Since the surface of lignin is rich in various functional groups, such as phenolic hydroxyl, alcohol hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, methoxy, etc, it can create favorable conditions for various reactions [29]. Furthermore, due to its high carbon content, long hydrophobic main chain, and hydrophilic side chains, lignin has a certain surface activity. The rich and unique phenolic hydroxyl groups in the structure can boost lignin utilization. Thus, carbon nanomaterials synthesized from lignin have the advantages of high efficiency, naturalness and biocompatibility.

Herein, inspired by the above research background, we innovatively synthesized a KCl-doped lignin carbon dots nanoenzymes (K,Cl-LCDs) with peroxidase activity and applied the nanomaterial for colorimetric detection of GSH. As shown in Scheme1, the K,Cl-LCDs are realized by the hydrothermal synthesis reaction of sodium lignosulfonate doped with KCl. K,Cl-LCDs catalyze the generation of hydroxyl radicals from H2O2, which undergoes a reaction that converts colorless 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to oxTMB. The reducing GSH is able to reduce the oxTMB, thus completing the detection of GSH concentration. Lignin nanoenzymes are used as reaction probes in this work, which reduces the restriction on natural enzymes. Moreover, based on the peroxidase activity of K,Cl-LCDs nanoenzymes, a colorimetric sensor that sensitively detects the concentration of GSH in human serum is designed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of K,Cl-LCDs by hydrothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: In the first step, 0.1 g KCl and 0.1 g sodium lignosulfonate were added into 20 mL distilled water, and the pretreated solution was obtained through a rapid blender. Next, the prepared solution was loaded into high pressure reactor and put into the electric drying oven. The temperature was set to 180 °C and kept for 12 h. Black K,Cl-LCDs solution was obtained at the end of the reaction. The prepared K,Cl-LCDs were centrifuged at 6500 rpm for 10 min to remove larger particles and then filtered with a 0.22 µm filter. After filtration, the solution was kept in a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 3500 Da in ultrapure water stirring for 24 h. The K,Cl-LCDs solutions outside the dialysis bag were further purified by permeation gel chromatography using a Sephadex LH-20 dextran gel column with methanol as the eluent. The collected fractions were rotary evaporated at 55 °C to obtain purified K,Cl-LCDs, and the liquid was then freeze-dried and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. The mass of solid obtained after lyophilization was 0.03 g, with a final yield of 15%. The K,Cl-LCDs used in the experiments were prepared in distilled water at a concentration of 3 mg mL−1.

Control variable method was applied to the steady-state kinetics of K,Cl-LCDs like peroxidases. At the first, the concentration of the fixed substrate TMB was set to 2.5 mM, and the concentration of H2O2 was changed to 0.125–1.25 mM. Next, when using 1.25 mM H2O2 as the substrate, the concentration of TMB varied from 0.25 mM to 2.5 mM. The other reaction conditions were exactly the same except the concentrations of these two substrates. After the reaction system was reacted for fixed time, the absorbance of the solution at 652 nm was detected. Finally, the Michaelis-Menten equation and Lineweaver-Burk equation were used to calculate the K
m and V
max of K,Cl-LCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric determination of GSH with K,Cl-LCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The signal for colorimetric detection was built on the characteristic absorption peak of the characteristic substrate oxTMB at 652 nm and the change of color. The colorimetric sensing system was set up as follows: 50 μL of 3 mg mL−1 K,Cl-LCDs, 50 μL of 0.125 mM H2O2 solution and 50 μL of 0.4 mM TMB with pH= 4 sodium acetate buffer to a volume of 2 mL. The reaction was carried out in the dark for 15 min at room temperature (25 °C) to allow the complete reaction of hydrogen peroxide catalyzed by K,Cl-LCDs and avoid the interference caused by the decomposition of H2O2 in the light. After the complete catalytic reaction of H2O2 by K,Cl-LCDs, different concentrations of GSH were added and kept for 20 min, then the absorbance at 652 nm was monitored.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay detection of GSH in serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: Human serum samples were obtained from the Northeast Forestry University Hospital. The samples were added with 10% trichloroacetic acid to remove serum proteins, centrifuged to obtain the supernatant, filtered through a 0.22 µm microporous membrane, and diluted 50 times with Tris-HCl buffer (50 mM, pH=8.0) solution for further use. The procedure for detecting human serum samples was the same as the above experiments. The concentration of GSH in human serum samples was calculated through linear equation.

Hydrothermal temperature, hydrothermal time, and the mass ratio of raw material to dopant influence the performance of K,Cl-LCDs. The enzymatic activity was studied in detail in the Supplementary Material with hydrothermal temperatures of 100–200 °C, hydrothermal times of 4–14 h, and dopant to carbon precursor mass ratios in the range of 0:1–2:1. After a comprehensive consideration, the best synthesis conditions were determined to be 180 °C, 12 h, and a dopant to carbon precursor mass ratio of 1:1. Sodium lignosulfonate acts as a carrier and as a surfactant [30] can enhance the dispersion of CDs; while KCl as a dopant can change the surface distribution of CDs. When the mass ratio of dopant to carbon precursor is low, the doping effect and modulation performance of KCl is not optimal and the catalytic activity is low. And when the mass ratio is large, the ionic strength is too high, which may cause too strong destruction of the electroneutrality in the carbon skeleton, and the CDs formed will be changed accordingly. As a result, the ideal doping ratio was determined to be 1:1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the inherent peroxidase K,Cl-LCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The structure of the K,Cl-LCDs was investigated to obtain morphology and particle size by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1A and B, the image captured the well-dispersed K,Cl-LCDs, and the particle size distribution is 2–6 nm. Besides, the HRTEM picture of a single K,Cl-LCDs revealed that the lattice fringe of the K,Cl-LCDs is 0.21 nm, which is consistent with the lattice spacing of the graphene (100) crystal plane [31]. The distribution of K and Cl elements is shown in the EDS spectrum (Fig. 3A), which proves that KCl is doped to the surface of the CDs. In addition, structural images at different pH are also shown in Fig. S2. It can be seen that the morphology and lattice fringes of K,Cl-LCDs do not change, which proves pH has almost no effect on the structure of K,Cl-LCDs.

FTIR was further used to investigate the functional groups in K,Cl-LCDs. Fig. 1C shows that K,Cl-LCDs have abundant functional groups on the surface. The strongest peak located at 1760 cm−1, corresponding to the CO stretching mode [32]. Other vibrational peaks are characteristic absorption peaks originating from C-O (1075 cm−1), -OH (3340 cm−1), and C-H (1413 cm−1 and 2976 cm−1), respectively. It is noteworthy that the vibrational peak of C-Cl appears at 667 cm−1 [33], indicating that the element Cl is doped into the surface structure of K,Cl-LCDs.

Fig. 1D reflects the XRD spectra of K,Cl-LCDs. There is a broad diffraction peak located at around 2θ= 20°, which corresponds to the c-axis lattice parameter of the graphite material [34], exhibiting that K,Cl-LCDs have an amorphous carbon structure.

XPS was used to analyze the elemental composition of K,Cl-LCDs. Fig. 2 shows the XPS full scan of K,Cl-LCDs and the peak splitting spectra of C, O, Cl, and K. The peaks of C1s, O1s and Cl2p are located at 285 eV, 532.2 eV and 200 eV, respectively. In detail, the high-resolution C1s can be deconvolved into four components, and the binding energy is 284.3 eV, 285.8 eV, 286.9 eV, 284.7 eV, which are attributed to CC, C-C, C-Cl/C-OH, and CO, respectively. The oxygen spectrum can be divided into an OC peak at 532.1 eV and a C-O peak at 532.8 eV. The fitted peak at 200.2 eV can represent the presence of Cl2p1/2 and Cl2p3/2 in the structure, which are located at 198.3 eV and 199.9 eV respectively [35]. In addition, the two peaks at binding energies of 292.8 eV and 377 eV characteristically represent the appearance of K2p3/2 and K2s. [36] Through above FTIR and XPS characterization results, it could be proved that KCl has been successfully doped on the surface of KCl-doped lignin carbon dots nanoenzymes.

In addition, the optical properties of K,Cl-LCDs were also characterized. It can be seen from Fig. 3B that K,Cl-LCDs exhibit excitation independence at excitation wavelengths of 330–380 nm. At the excitation wavelength of 390–410 nm, the excitation correlation is exhibited, and the position of the emission peak is red-shifted with the movement of the excitation wavelength. It is speculated that the formation of this excitation-dependent behavior is due to the synergistic effect of surface defect states and carbon nucleus states of K,Cl-LCDs. The effect of pH on the PL intensity of K,Cl-LCD is also discussed. PL spectra were recorded over the pH range of 3–12. As shown in Fig. 3 C, the PL intensities and emission wavelengths of K,Cl-LCDs varied less in the pH range of 3–9. However, when the solution is alkaline, the PL intensity decreases significantly and the emission position also shifts. It is speculated that the protonation effect may lead to the change of the electronic state. PL spectra were also recorded under various concentrations (0.02–0.2 M) [25] of sodium chloride salts, showing a substantially constant response of PL intensity, with no aggregation of K,Cl-LCDs in the presence of ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase mimetic behavior of K,Cl-LCDs and sensing properties optimization
ce:section.ce:para: The enzyme-like activity of K,Cl-LCDs, and the optimal sensing properties were explored. The effect of pH in the range of 3.0–6.5 and temperature in the range of 5–55 °C on enzyme activity was investigated. Fig. S3 shows that K,Cl-LCDs have peroxidase activity, the strongest catalytic activity at pH= 4, 25 °C. When the pH value is higher than 5.5, H2O2 will be decomposed, resulting in the weakening of the oxidation reaction to TMB [37]. The diamine structure of TMB showed poor solubility in weak alkaline medium [38], so the enzyme-like catalytic activity of K,Cl-LCDs is lower at high pH value. Besides, K,Cl-LCDs could complete the enzyme-like catalytic reaction within 15 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of steady-state kinetic studies of K,Cl-LCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Steady-state kinetics was used to further characterize the catalytic activity. Fig. 4 depicts the image of the V
max of the reaction system in relation to the different substrates. The catalytic capacity of K,Cl-LCDs shows a trend of increasing and then leveling off when the concentrations of the two substrates are increased, respectively. In addition, taking the inverse of the substrate concentration and the maximum velocity as the horizontal and vertical coordinates respectively, an image of the Lineweaver-Burk relationship can be obtained with a good linear relationship between 1/V and 1/[S]. The K
m and V
max can thus be calculated to be 0.85 mM and 2.51 × 10−6 M s−1 for TMB as substrate and 0.19 mM and 11.63 × 10−8 M s−1 for H2O2 as substrate, respectively. As shown in Table S1, by comparing the kinetic behavior parameters of other reported peroxidase-like materials, it is concluded that the synthesized K,Cl-LCDs has a low K
m value and a higher V
max for H2O2. Thus, K,Cl-LCDs have better affinity for H2O2. Moreover, the kinetics of K,Cl-LCDs are further studied. As Fig. S4 shown, the Lineweaver-Burk lines of the two substrates were found to be parallel when their concentrations were changed individually, suggesting that the reaction of K,Cl-LCDs catalyzing H2O2 follows a ping-pong mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the catalytic mechanism of peroxidase-like
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the mechanism of K,Cl-LCDs peroxidase catalyzing H2O2, several free radical scavengers were utilized to explore the kinds of free radicals produced during the catalysis process. In the catalytic reactions, isopropyl alcohol (IPA) solution was used to remove hydroxyl groups (·OH) from the system [24], 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ) with a concentration of 10 mM was used to remove superoxide ions (O2 -) from the system [39] and a concentration of 10 mM tryptophan (Try) was used to remove singlet oxygen (1O2) from the system [40]. It is acknowledged that the absorbance value of the system decreases when the corresponding radicals are captured by the radical scavengers in the system. Only the absorbance of the system containing isopropyl alcohol decreased, whereas the absorbance of the system including other free radical scavengers remained steady, showing that ·OH is produced in the system. The absorbance of the K,Cl-LCDs-TMB-H2O2 system decreases as the amount of isopropanol increases, as seen in Fig. 5C. Besides, the PL properties of terephthalic acid (TA) were used to verify the electron transfer between K,Cl-LCDs and H2O2. When TA (5 mM) reacts with the ·OH generated by the decomposition of H2O2, the generated hydroxy-terephthalic acid will have a characteristic emission peak around 420 nm under the excitation of 315 nm. As shown in Fig. 5B, the PL intensity of the emission peak at 420 nm gradually increased with the increase of time, proving that the ·OH generated by catalyzation by K,Cl-LCDs was captured by TA [41].

The mechanism is further supported by the energy spectrum of the Electron Spin Resonance (ESR). For ·OH, DMPO is used as a spin trapping agent. When ·OH is trapped, a DMPO/·OH spin adduct is formed, which is a relatively stable paramagnetic product with a typical ESR signal of 1:2:2:1. The ESR energy spectra of three sets of systems are represented by the three curves in Fig. 5D: H2O2 + DMPO+ K,Cl-LCDs; H2O2 + DMPO and K,Cl-LCDs + DMPO. Among them, the energy spectra of H2O2 + DMPO+ K,Cl-LCDs systems shows the typical signal of DMPO/·OH spin adduct, representing the generation of ·OH in the system. In addition, the zeta potential was used to characterize the surface charge of the prepared K,Cl-LCDs with the substrate TMB. Fig. 5E reflects the K,Cl-LCDs have a modest negative potential, whereas TMB has a positive potential. As a result of electrostatic interactions, K,Cl-LCDs could attract positively charged TMB substrates and thus better oxidize TMB by catalyzing the H2O2 reaction.

The mechanism of peroxidase activity inherent in K,Cl-LCDs was also discussed. The size of K,Cl-LCDs is small by TEM particle size analysis. It is reported that the small size of nanoparticles has a larger specific surface area, which can generate more potential active sites and provide the necessary basis for the improved catalytic performance of the nanoenzymes. There are several oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of sodium lignosulfonate, such as ether bonds, phenolic hydroxyl groups, and so on. In the hydrothermal process, an oxidation reaction occurs, the doping of KCl will change the surface state, and the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of sodium lignosulfonate are converted into CO and COOH. The CO and COOH were confirmed to be the catalytically active and binding sites of peroxidase-like nanoenzymes [14]. The rate-determining step of the CO reaction is to be attacked by H2O2 to generate an intermediate product, which is easily decomposed into ·OH, so CO is the binding site. Due to the interaction of hydrogen bonds, H2O2 is easily bound to the COOH, so the COOH is the main binding site of H2O2 [41]. According to the above-mentioned FTIR spectra and XPS spectra of K,Cl-LCDs, it can be concluded that K,Cl-LCDs contains these two functional groups on the surface. It is speculated that the activity of K,Cl-LCDs is also attributed to these two functional groups. At the same time, the electron transfer process is highly related to the catalytic reaction of nanoenzymes, electron transfer from K,Cl-LCDs to H2O2 is attributed to the increase in Fermi level electron density. Electrons are transferred from the lone pair of electrons on the amino group of TMB to the surface of K,Cl-LCDs, promoting the electron transfer process of K,Cl-LCDs to H2O2, thereby accelerating the reaction of TMB being oxidized to blue in the presence of H2O2. From the above study of the radical species in the system, it is obtained that the catalytic reaction of K,Cl-LCDs is a hydroxyl radical reaction induced by the Fenton reaction. That is, H2O2 first combines with K,Cl-LCDs on the surface, K,Cl-LCDs catalyzes H2O2 to produce ·OH and OH-, then the produced ·OH undergoes a redox reaction with TMB to produce oxTMB. Potassium is a kind of alkali metal element and can be used as a dopant to realize n-type doping of CDs. We speculate that during the synthesis of CDs, the doping of potassium metal element accelerates the fluctuations and transitions of atoms, changes the steric hindrance, and promotes the electron-hole recombination process, resulting in the change of the Fermi level. The interfacial charge transfer ability of CDs is improved. In addition, halogens play an important role in the adsorption and reduction of H2O2. The presence of Cl- can accelerate the process of electron loss from H2O2 to ·OH [42], and the doping of K element can affect the electron transfer process of K,Cl-LCDs to the substrate. The co-doping of K element and Cl element can change the electron transfer capability of K,Cl-LCDs and further enhance the peroxidase-like catalytic activity [43].

In addition, density functional theory (DFT) was used to explain its catalytic properties. Based on the elemental content and functional groups involved in XPS and FTIR, we derived the approximate structure of K,Cl-LCDs as shown in Fig. S5. The Mulliken charge distribution as well as the HUMO and LUMO orbitals of the structure were calculated for the target using Gaussian 09 software with B3LYP/6–311 +G as the reference. The calculated atomic charges, HUMO, LUMO values are listed in Table S2 and Fig. S5, which may help to understand the catalytic properties of K,Cl-LCDs. The value of ΔE (EHUMO-ELUMO) was calculated to be 1.73 eV, with lower ΔE representing higher activity. Furthermore, by evaluating the charge number of each atom in the electronic structure, it was learned that the charge numbers of the carbonyl groups near K and Cl tended to decrease after KCl doping and making the potential values negative, such as oxygen atoms 60, 72 and 73. It reflects that the addition of KCl could improve the catalytic performance.

As a powerful antioxidant, GSH can promote the reduction of oxTMB. The time for detecting GSH was optimized, as shown in Fig. 6A, and according to the results, 20 min was determined as the time when the GSH reaction was complete. By comparing the time for GSH detection in other reports, 20 min in this method is better than some of them, and has the advantages of simplicity and low cost of raw materials for environmental protection. The absorbance of the solution system containing oxTMB at 652 nm gradually decreases with the increase of GSH concentration. As given view in Fig. 6B, the absorbance of the system is proportional to the concentration of GSH in the range of 0.25–25 μM, showing a good linearity. The linear regression equation is y = −0.0235x+ 1.2281, in which y indicates the absorbance at 652 nm and x indicates the concentration of the GSH. Calculated by the tripling signal-to-noise ratio, the detection limit of K,Cl-LCDs for detecting GSH is 0.08 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic stability study of K,Cl-LCDs and selective research
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of K,Cl-LCDs was investigated by testing their catalytic activity three times a week for eight weeks. As shown in Fig. S6A, the difference is relatively small, making clear that K,Cl-LCDs have high stability. The effects of common proteins, amino acids, and ions in serum on K,Cl-LCDs sensors were also examined. The result (Fig. S6B) shows that AA and L-cys interfere with the selectivity of the system to a certain extent, but the concentrations of these two substances in human serum are much lower than the concentration of GSH [44], and the interference is very small, so K,Cl-LCDs have good selectivity in the detection of actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of spiked GSH in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the practical feasibility of K,Cl-LCDs-TMB-H2O2 based GSH sensing in real samples, diluted human serum was investigated. After testing and calculations, the GSH concentrations in the three human serum samples were 681, 488 and 530 μM, respectively. Meanwhile, these samples were also assayed with an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) -based GSH assay kit, and the results were 675, 480 and 541 μM, respectively, which are substantially similar with the proposed method's measurements. Compared with the natural peroxidase used in the kit, K,Cl-LCDs have the advantages of low cost, high stability and easy preparation. The spiked recovery experiments were further performed with the results given in Table 1. The recoveries of the GSH were 98.2%− 103.2% with the relative standard deviations of 2.3%− 4.7%, indicating that the sensor based on the K,Cl-LCDs-TMB-H2O2 system can be successfully applied to the determination of bio-real samples.

Several different methods for the detection of GSH are given in Table S3. By comparison, the recovery and RSD of the method based on K,Cl-LCDs were found to be similar to those of the other methods. In terms of LOD and linear range, it is slightly better than most of the methods but inferior to the electrochemical detection method. However, the electrochemical method uses graphene oxide prepared with potassium permanganate and concentrated sulfuric acid, which is a dangerous process to prepare and operate. The colorimetric method is more intuitive than the electrochemical and fluorescence methods. Wang et al. [45] also used a nanoenzyme colorimetric method for GSH detection as well, however the nanoenzyme in this method is modified gold nanoparticles, which is an expensive and time-consuming procedure. In the proposed method, sodium lignosulfonate and KCl, which are low-cost, environmentally friendly, and easy to make, are used to convert low-value lignin into a high-value nanoenzyme, enhancing its use in the detection of GSH in human serum samples.

In this research, K,Cl-LCDs obtained from sodium lignosulfonate and KCl by hydrothermal synthesis are used as peroxidase-like enzymes for the first time. The rich functional groups on the surface of sodium lignosulfonate can be used as active sites and KCl can be used as modifiers. It can be concluded that the catalytic reaction of K,Cl-LCDs nanoenzymes is based on the ·OH. In addition, based on K,Cl-LCDs, a sensor for colorimetric detection of GSH is constructed with low detection limit (0.08 μM) and good selectivity. The constructed colorimetric sensor can also be well applied to the detection of GSH in human serum samples. What’s more, the colorimetric sensor based on K,Cl-LCDs to detect GSH is expected to be further applied in biomedicine and tumor treatment.

Xiaoyu Xu: Methodology, Data Curation, Writing – original draft. Qijun Sun: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Yanmei Ma: Investigation, Validation. Xinxin Jiang: Validation. Na Niu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Ligang Chen: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was funded by Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572021BU03), 111 Project (B20088), Heilongjiang Touyan Innovation Team Program (Tree Genetics and Breeding Innovation Team).

ce:section.ce:para: All applicable international, national, and/or institutional guidelines for the collection and use of human blood and serum samples were followed. All biological experiments were approved by the Ethics Committee of Harbin Medical University Cancer Hospital.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.131881.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

Chem. 410 (2018) 4805–4813. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00216–018-1117–4.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiaoyu Xu is a master student at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. Her major is chemistry and her research direction is nanoenzymes biological analysis.

Qijun Sun is a Doctor candidate at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. His major is biology and his research direction is nanoenzymes biological analysis.
Yanmei Ma is a master student at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. Her research direction is extraction and purification

Xinxin Jiang is a master student at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. Her major is biology and her research direction is chemical biology.

Na Niu is an associate professor at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. Her main research interests are in multifunctional materials-based bioapplications.

Ligang Chen is a professor at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. His main research directions are biosensing probes and molecular imprinting techniques.
